data.title: CFTC launches probe into suspicious oil futures trading on CME, ICE - report
data.description:
The Commodity Futures Trading Commission has launched an investigation into a series of suspiciously well-timed trades in the oil futures market, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg.

The derivatives regulator is leading a probe into the trading of oil futures contracts on CME Group (CME) and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) platforms, according to the people who asked not to be identified as the information is private.

The two exchanges were asked to share related data, with the requested data including the so-called Tag 50 identifications of the entities behind the trades, the Wednesday, April 15 report noted.

Last week, senators Elizabeth Warren and Sheldon Whitehouse were said to be pushing for an investigation into unusual trading patterns in oil futures, noticed immediately preceding two major government announcements related to the Iran conflict.

Warren had sent a letter to Michael Selig, chairman of the CFTC, regarding a dramatic surge in oil futures trading on March 23 and April 7.

data.title: PSEG cut to Hold at Jefferies on lower confidence in data center deal
data.description:
Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) down 1.3% in Wednesday's trading as Jefferies downgraded the utility to Hold from Buy with an $89 price target, citing lower confidence in a nuclear data center deal as New Jersey's ratepayer protection pledge reduces hyperscaler interest in contracting with existing assets without new generation.

Jefferies analyst Paul Zimbardo said he had been hopeful that PSEG (PEG) would sign a virtual power purchase agreement and credit customers back some of the value, which is still a possibility but "we are not willing to ascribe material value" with a more intense focus on affordability; the company is helping pursue new nuclear at its site, but the analyst does not see this increasing the odds of securing an on-campus data center.

Investors seem increasingly focused on PSEG's (PEG) transmission exposure with ~38% of FY 2025 rate base following the New England cut; Zimbardo said he sees the company's 9.9% base return on equity as safe but with vulnerability to legislation that would mandate RTO participation—"not "our base case, but this is an overhang on shares that is difficult to refute."

PSEG's (PEG) strong story was derailed by high electric rates in 2025 and becoming the center of the election cycle with New Jersey Governor Sherrill focusing her attention on a utility rate freeze; "a solid utility story now needs to work within the confines of a less accommodating regime, leading to a slower pace of investments and increasing reliance on unregulated earnings growth," the analyst wrote.

data.title: H.B. Fuller dividend preview: set for another dividend increase
data.description:
H.B. Fuller Company (FUL) is expected to announce a dividend hike in mid-April, extending its 56-year dividend growth streak based on historical trends.

Analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of $0.97 per share, implying a quarterly dividend of $0.2425, which would represent a 3.2% increase from the prior payout of $0.235.

The company last paid a dividend of $0.235 per share in February 2026, representing an annual yield of 1.49%, and raised its dividend last April by 5.6% to $0.235 from $0.225.

The specialty materials company has delivered a 5-year dividend growth rate of approximately 7.7% and maintains a 4-year average payout ratio of 21.05%.

The company holds ratings of B for safety, B- for growth, C for yield, and A+ for dividend consistency.

data.title: Asml signals 2026 revenue of €36B-€40B while raising low-NA EUV capacity to at least 80 systems in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "In the first quarter of 2026, total net sales were EUR 8.8 billion" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board R.J.M. Dassen), and "gross margin for the quarter was at the high end of our guidance at 53%" while "net income in Q1 was EUR 2.8 billion... resulting in earnings per share of EUR 7.15."
  • "Our Q1 free cash flow was negative EUR 2.6 billion, largely driven by the timing of down payments" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), while the quarter ended with "cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments... EUR 8.4 billion."
  • "The growth outlook for the semiconductor industry continues to solidify, driven primarily by AI-related infrastructure investment" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Christophe Fouquet), adding that "for the foreseeable future, demand will continue to outpace supply" and that customers are "aggressively add[ing] capacity."
  • "We are executing on an output plan of at least 60 low-NA EUV system" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet) and said ASML is "raising low-NA EUV capacity to at least 80 systems next year" alongside scaling deep UV and applications.

Outlook

  • "We expect Q2 total net sales to be between EUR 8.4 billion and EUR 9 billion" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), with "installed base management sales... around EUR 2.5 billion" and "gross margin for Q2... between 51% and 52% (sic) [53%]."
  • "We are... updating our 2026 guidance, both narrowing and increasing the expected revenue range to between EUR 36 billion and EUR 40 billion" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet), while "maintaining our expectation of a gross margin between 51% and 53%, with revenue weighted to the second half of the year."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s full-year view of "between EUR 34 billion and EUR 39 billion" revenue and "gross margin between 51% and 53%" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen; Q4 call), the 2026 revenue range was raised and narrowed in Q1.
  • "The bandwidth in our 2026 guidance accommodates potential outcomes of ongoing discussions around export control" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet).

Financial results

  • "Net system sales were EUR 6.3 billion" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), including "over EUR 4.1 billion from EUV system sales, including sales from 2 High NA systems" and "over EUR 2.1 billion from non-EUV system sales."
  • "Installed Base Management sales for the quarter came in at EUR 2.5 billion" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), and he attributed Q1 gross margin strength "primarily due to the contribution of very high-margin components within our installed base business."
  • "R&D expenses were around at EUR 1.2 billion and SG&A expenses came in at EUR 0.3 billion" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), and "the effective tax rate for Q1 was 17.1%."
  • "Net system sales were almost equally split between Logic at 49% and Memory at 51%" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), as management described AI-driven demand lifting both advanced logic and memory capacity additions.

Q&A

  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: asked how much of higher immersion/deep UV outlook is China vs. non-China and whether EUV changed; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen replied, "This is primarily non-China when it comes to immersion" and "China remains at the midpoint around 20%... that view has not changed," adding "in EUV, we believe there is a bit more that can be done."
  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: asked why gross margin range stayed unchanged with more immersion; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen said, "we're increasing the move rate quite a bit" and "hiring people... training people that also costs a bit of money."
  • Sreekrishnan Sankarnarayanan, TD Cowen: asked about visibility into 2028 and growth in 2027/2028; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet answered, "2028 is still quite far away" and pointed to "preparing to build at least 80 Low NA EUV system next year" as the current 2027 signal.
  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: pressed on Q2 margin drivers; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen said Q1 had "very high-margin elements in the upgrade business" and "We're not counting on such a strong pattern... in Q2" alongside headcount ramp costs.
  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked how ASML drives extra supply and supply-chain constraints; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet said preparation toward "a capability of 90 on Low NA and... 600 for deep UV" is "paying off" and tool maturity is enabling "shorter cycle time" in the factory.
  • Sandeep Deshpande, JPMorgan: asked whether deep UV capacity is enough as EUV rises; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet answered, "we still feel pretty good about" 600 total deep UV capacity and said immersion demand "will scale with the demand for EUV."
  • Sandeep Deshpande, JPMorgan: asked about EUV mix/ASPs next year; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen said for 2027 "hardly, if any, Ds" and "there will, of course, be Fs," concluding, "the ASP next year will improve over this year."
  • Francois-Xavier Bouvignies, UBS: asked if tightness could accelerate High NA adoption; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet responded, "it's a bit too early" but "I will not exclude that."
  • Didier Scemama, BofA: asked if prior CMD wafer-start assumptions still hold; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet said, "things have changed a bit with AI" and "the biggest change is DRAM," adding ASML will likely revisit at "the Capital Market Day" next year.
  • Alexander Duval, Goldman Sachs: asked about contributions from additional foundry players and hybrid bonding; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen said foundry demand "is huge and is outweighing the supply" and noted Samsung’s "plans... in Taylor," while President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet said ASML sees "3D integration" as important and highlighted "wafer-to-wafer bonding" and "entering advanced packaging with the XT:260."
  • Christopher Caso, Wolfe: asked about customer clean-room constraints; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet said "the clean room capacity was one of the factor... on what we could do this year" but added 2026 plans are "solidifying."
  • Tammy Qiu, Berenberg: asked if tighter memory market changes pricing; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen said, "our model of pricing is not based on the squeeze" and is based on "the value that we provide."
  • Lee Simpson, Morgan Stanley: asked if installed-base outperformance was mostly upgrades and sustainability; Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen responded, "we expect upgrade business to continue throughout the year" but said Q1 benefited from "very, very high gross margins" items within upgrades.

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone focused on constraints and durability, with repeated probing on mix/margins, customer capacity, and China exposure (e.g., "How much of that is coming from China versus non-China?" Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo; and "Could you speak about... clean room constraints" Christopher Caso, Wolfe).
  • Slightly positive management tone emphasizing strong demand and execution, including "we continue to see a strong year ahead" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet) and margin explanations framed as ramp investments rather than demand weakness.
  • Versus the prior quarter’s “video” format that repeatedly described conditions as "a strong quarter" and a market that "has notably improved" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen; President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet), Q1 carried a more operational tone with more detailed pushback on margins, supply, and clean-room readiness.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from Q4’s 2026 revenue "between EUR 34 billion and EUR 39 billion" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen; Q4 call) to Q1’s "between EUR 36 billion and EUR 40 billion" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet), while keeping the 51%-53% full-year gross margin range.
  • Strategic emphasis broadened from Q4’s expectation that non-EUV would be "flattish" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen; Q4 call) to Q1’s statement that "we now expect growth" in non-EUV revenue, driven by deep UV demand (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet).
  • Analysts in Q1 concentrated more on supply mechanics (move rates, headcount, clean rooms, supply chain) and the near-term margin cadence; Q4 included fewer investor questions in the transcript provided.

Risks and concerns

  • "The bandwidth in our 2026 guidance accommodates potential outcomes of ongoing discussions around export control" (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet), while Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen reiterated, "China remains at the midpoint around 20%."
  • "Increasing the move rate means that you're hiring people... training people that also costs a bit of money" (Executive VP, CFO & Member of the Management Board Dassen), which management linked to near-term margin pressure even as volumes rise.
  • "The clean room capacity was one of the factor" affecting 2026 delivery pacing (President, CEO and Chair of the Board of Management Fouquet), though he said customer plans are "solidifying" as pedestal availability becomes clearer.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as within plan on revenue and at the high end of margin guidance, while pointing to AI-driven demand that it said is outpacing supply across advanced logic and memory. For 2026, management raised and narrowed its revenue range to €36B-€40B while keeping the 51%-53% gross margin range, and framed the higher outlook as driven by stronger immersion/deep UV execution outside China, incremental EUV opportunity, and installed-base upgrades. In Q&A, executives repeatedly stressed that capacity expansion is being pursued via unit output, tool productivity, and installed-base upgrades, while also acknowledging constraints from hiring/ramp costs, customer clean-room readiness, and uncertainty from export-control discussions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bank of America raises 2026 NII growth outlook to 6%-8% as it targets 200+ bps operating leverage
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bank of America (BAC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Bank of America delivered strong first quarter 2026 results. Revenue grew 7% year-over-year to $30.3 billion. Earnings per share were up 25% year-over-year to $1.11 per share." (Chairman & CEO Brian Moynihan)
  • "Net interest income performed better than expected. On an FTE basis, net interest income was $15.9 billion, up 9% year-over-year." (Chairman & CEO Moynihan)
  • "We reported noninterest expense of $18.5 billion in the first quarter, which was in line with the roughly 4% year-over-year increase we discussed on our last quarterly earnings call." (Chairman & CEO Moynihan)
  • "Headcount, we are down about 1,070 people from year-end 2025 through attrition, and we'll continue to drive that." (Chairman & CEO Moynihan)
  • "This quarter, we paid $2 billion in common dividends and we bought back $7.2 billion of common shares." (Executive VP & CFO Alastair Borthwick)
  • "Deposits increased to more than $2 trillion" and "the CET1 capital ratio declined 14 basis points to 11.2%." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)

Outlook

  • "Given our outperformance against expectations of NII in Q1 and based on the most recent interest rate curve, which has now shifted from 2 rate cuts expected to having none currently, we're raising our full year NII growth guidance range for 2026 versus 2025 to be up 6% to 8%." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)
  • "Looking ahead, we continue to expect more than 200 basis points of positive operating leverage for the year, consistent with our prior guidance." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)
  • "We expect an effective tax rate of just a little more than 20%." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)

Financial Results

  • "Revenue grew 7% year-over-year to $30.3 billion" and "Earnings per share were up 25% year-over-year to $1.11 per share." (Chairman & CEO Brian Moynihan)
  • "In the first quarter, net interest income on a fully taxable equivalent basis was $15.9 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Alastair Borthwick)
  • "Net charge-offs were approximately $1.4 billion with a net loss rate of 48 basis points" and "Provision expense was approximately $1.3 billion, including a modest net reserve release." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)
  • "Consumer Banking delivered a strong first quarter" with "Net income ... $3.1 billion" and "record 38.5 million consumer checking accounts." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)
  • "Global Wealth and Investment Management" reported "record first quarter revenue of $6.7 billion" and "Client balances increased to $4.6 trillion." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)
  • "Global Markets" reported "Revenues ex DVA were $7 billion" and "Equities had their best quarter ever with revenues up 30% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick)

Q&A

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked how better NII flows through while keeping expense and operating leverage; Chairman & CEO Moynihan: "We did this quarter, and we expect that the NII will drop to the bottom line."
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: asked why consumer loan and deposit growth is "sluggish"; Executive VP & CFO Borthwick: "it looks like we're finding that floor and beginning to grow out of it" and "we could put up more deposit growth if we wanted to, but we're really choosing... to just maximize the core operating client account activity."
  • Glenn Schorr, Evercore ISI: asked about headcount actions and AI; Chairman & CEO Moynihan: "we are down about 1,070 people" and "we've got 90 installations working, all 200,000 teammates have access to AI or can use it every day."
  • John McDonald, Truist: asked about capital buffers and targets; Executive VP & CFO Borthwick: "we're gaining a little more confidence" and Chairman & CEO Moynihan: "expect us to keep it in the 50 basis points that we said."
  • James Mitchell, Seaport: asked funding optimization capacity; Executive VP & CFO Borthwick: "probably somewhere around $100 billion or so" (remaining), and on C&I: "It's mostly credit line utilization this quarter" with "$5 billion to $10 billion" from revolver draws.
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: asked whether BofA is an AI beneficiary; Chairman & CEO Moynihan: "we are a beneficiary" and later added "99% round numbers of all the interactions we have with our consumers are digital already."
  • Saul Martinez, HSBC: challenged reserve release amid uncertainty; Executive VP & CFO Borthwick: "we took 5% out of the upside case" and said reserve movement was driven by "continued improvement in CRE office."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on longer-term durability and risk topics, including AI disruption, consumer deposit/loan momentum, trading durability, capital buffers, and reserving; for example, Saul Martinez said he was "a little bit surprised" by a reserve release. (HSBC)
  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident in delivery and controls, repeatedly emphasizing flow-through and discipline; for example, Chairman & CEO Moynihan said "we expect that the NII will drop to the bottom line" and Executive VP & CFO Borthwick said "I don't think there's any one-timers to consider here."
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call included a clearer upward shift in NII guidance language (now "raising" the range) while maintaining operating leverage expectations, alongside more Q&A attention on AI and reserve posture.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 raised the 2026 NII growth outlook to "up 6% to 8%" (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick) versus Q4’s "5% to 7% growth" view for 2026 compared to 2025 (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick).
  • Q1 described the forward curve change as "shifted from 2 rate cuts expected to having none currently" (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick) versus Q4’s outlook that "includes two rate cuts in 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick).
  • Q1 capital return accelerated to "bought back $7.2 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick) versus Q4’s "$6.3 billion" of repurchases (Executive VP & CFO Borthwick).
  • Q1 Q&A leaned more heavily into AI as a strategic debate and deposit stickiness, while Q4’s Q&A placed heavier emphasis on expense ratio messaging, stablecoins, and the proposed card yield cap.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We also are mindful of all the risks out there, the ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, including implications for the energy market inflation and growth." (Chairman & CEO Brian Moynihan)
  • "Regarding interest rate sensitivity... an additional 100 basis point decline in rates beyond the forward curve, would reduce NII over the next 12 months by $2 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Alastair Borthwick)
  • On private credit and Global Markets loans, Executive VP & CFO Borthwick said "we acknowledge the potential for underwriting dispersion" but added: "Bank of America's exposure has structural insulation" and "We don't rely on sponsor marks. We re-underwrite collateral continuously."

Final Takeaway

Bank of America’s management emphasized Q1 momentum across segments, led by $30.3 billion of revenue and $1.11 EPS, and highlighted that net interest income of $15.9 billion was better than expected. The most stock-relevant update was CFO Alastair Borthwick’s increase to full-year 2026 NII growth guidance to 6% to 8% (from prior 5% to 7%), tied to a shift in the rate-cut outlook and continued balance growth, while reiterating expectations for 200+ basis points of operating leverage. In Q&A, analysts pressed on capital buffers, AI disruption, consumer growth pacing, and reserving, with management repeatedly stating that incremental NII is expected to flow to the bottom line and that underwriting and risk posture remain unchanged.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. Army awards Boeing $324M contract for more Chinook helicopters
data.description:
The U.S. Army awarded Boeing (BA) a $324 million contract for six additional CH-47F Block II Chinook helicopters, expanding its existing order as part of a broader effort to accelerate delivery of key equipment.

The latest award brings the total number of CH-47F Block II aircraft under contract to 24. The agreement follows earlier orders placed last year, including the first tied to the Army’s rapid fielding initiative aimed at speeding deployment timelines.

Boeing (BA) has already delivered six of the upgraded Chinook helicopters. The Army plans to equip its first operational unit with the aircraft by mid-2028, following additional deliveries tied to earlier production lots.

The CH-47F Block II is an updated version of the long-serving Chinook platform, designed primarily for heavy-lift transport and logistics missions. The aircraft includes structural and mechanical changes that increase its maximum weight capacity, allowing it to carry heavier loads.

Other modifications include adjustments to the fuel system that extend operating range, as well as design changes intended to simplify maintenance and allow for future upgrades.

The Chinook has been a central component of the Army’s rotorcraft fleet for decades, supporting cargo transport and resupply missions in a range of operating environments.

data.title: Fashion moves: Walmart launches a collection tied to The Devil Wears Prada
data.description:
Walmart (WMT) is moving back into the spotlight with a new movie‑inspired fashion drop. The retail giant is unveiling a Scoop capsule collection tied to The Devil Wears Prada.

The limited-time capsule collection, which aims to channel the film’s high‑gloss New York editorial aesthetic into wearable, budget-friendly pieces, debuts on April 20. The line features tailored blazers, matching suit pants, structured dresses, red pumps, and statement belts.

The line is being positioned as a statement that Walmart intends to compete more directly on style, not just on everyday low prices. Over the past few years, Walmart (WMT) has steadily upgraded its apparel offerings, leaning on private brands like Scoop, Free Assembly, and Sofia Jeans to offer more modern silhouettes, on‑trend fabrics, and curated outfits that resemble what shoppers might expect from department stores or specialty chains rather than a traditional big‑box player.

The Devil Wears Prada is a classic 2006 American comedy‑drama film based on Lauren Weisberger’s novel. The movie follows Andy Sachs (played by Anne Hathaway), a young aspiring journalist who takes an assistant job at the prestigious fashion magazine Runway and finds herself working for formidable editor Miranda Priestly (played by Meryl Streep), discovering the pressures and temptations of the high-fashion world. The upcoming sequel, The Devil Wears Prada 2, is scheduled to debut on May 1.

data.title: Marvell sees bullish views at Oppenheimer on growth outlook
data.description:
Oppenheimer raised Marvell Technology's (MRVL) price target to $170 from $150 following a meeting with company executives, who were bullish on the growth outlook for AI data center networking and application-specific integrated circuits, or ASIC. 

The firm kept its Outperform rating on Marvell's stock. Shares of the company dipped about 1% on Wednesday.

Analysts led by Rick Schafer said they hosted investor meetings in Europe with Marvell's Chairman and CEO Matt Murphy and Ashish Saran, senior vice president of Investor Relations.

"Tone was unequivocally bullish as mgmt. highlighted accelerating growth outlook led by DCAI [Data Center AI] networking and ASIC. Top line expected $11B-plus this year (up from ~$9B six months ago), $15B+ in CY27. DC, 75% of sales, splits 70/30 networking/ASIC near term. We see mix gradually moving toward 60/40 longer term as XPU/XPU [Extended Processing Unit] attach wins fold in. Together, custom AI ASIC sales expected to at least double next year to $4B before hitting $10B+ in CY28. Optics has grown 50% annually for five years," said Schafer and his team.

The analysts see Marvell's share as stable to increasing across core networking markets (Pulse Amplitude Modulation 4-level, or PAM4, scale out/up switching, etc.) and look for the segment to lead upside if their capital expenditure, or CAPEX, assumptions prove conservative.

Schafer and his team added that supply, not demand, remains the growth governor. While advanced wafer capacity remains the primary upside bottleneck, the analysts believe management can build ahead in the company's merchant Application-Specific Standard Product, or ASSP, (networking) franchise.

In addition, the analysts said that lead times stretched to nine to 12 months, though they do not see component shortages curbing upside. They believe data center build-outs (reined in by things like grid capacity and increased resistance from locals) are the actual long tent poles.

Networking interconnects are expected to be up more than 50% this year to over $5B. Switch more than $600M and ASICs over $2B this year, according to the analysts. 

"Networking TAM [Total Addressable Market] aperture expanding, juiced by recent $2B NVDA partnership. Scale-up switch TAM forecast $30B in 2030, split 2/3 NVDA (NVLink), 1/3 merchant (UALink, ESUN). NVDA partnership allows MRVL to address all three protocols. No need to call a “winner” today. MRVL content (Celestial’s photonic fabric, for example) on both sides of the cable (switch+XPU) triples company scale-up TAM to $90B (2030). MRVL the Switzerland of interconnect, well positioned with a full suite copper/optical portfolio. MRVL's diverse, structural growth led by DCAI share/content wins," said Schafer and his team.

data.title: GE Aerospace, India's HAL advance landmark jet engine co-production deal
data.description:
Indian and U.S. firms have moved closer to a landmark agreement to jointly build jet engines, as Prime Minister Narendra Modi pushes to upgrade the country’s air force and expand domestic weapons production.

Ohio-based GE Aerospace (GE) and state-run Hindustan Aeronautics Limited said Tuesday they have finalized key technical terms for the co-production of F414 engines, bringing a long-gestating project nearer to execution. Discussions between the two sides have stretched roughly three years.

The arrangement includes sharing manufacturing know-how with India, with a formal contract expected to follow. New Delhi has also explored similar partnerships with companies in France, Japan and the United Kingdom as it looks to deepen its domestic industrial base.

Both companies said the agreement builds on a decades-long relationship and represents a step toward closer economic and defense ties between India and the United States.

The F414 engines are slated to power India’s next-generation, domestically produced fighter jets. The country aims to field roughly 120 to 130 of these aircraft to replace aging Russian-origin fleets. The engine model has been in service with the U.S. Navy for more than three decades.

The initiative supports Modi’s broader effort to strengthen military readiness and address a shrinking number of operational fighter squadrons, a key concern given ongoing tensions with Pakistan and China.

India’s air capabilities came under scrutiny following clashes with Pakistan last year, when Islamabad said its Chinese-built J-10C aircraft downed multiple Indian jets.

Momentum for the co-production effort dates back to 2023, when then-President Joe Biden and Modi agreed to expand cooperation on advanced defense systems, including jet engines, as well as emerging technologies such as semiconductors and quantum computing.

data.title: Nixxy reports 2025 revenue of $98M; shares slipped over 11%
data.description:
• Nixxy (NIXX) reported on Wednesday approximately $98M in revenue for fiscal 2025. 
  • The company reported a net loss of approximately $15M in 2025, compared to $22.6M in 2024, reflecting improving operating dynamics as the communications platform expanded. A portion of the reported loss reflects non-cash accounting items, stock-based compensation, and costs associated with the strategic transition and separation of legacy operations.
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss for 2025 was approximately $5.6M, excluding non-cash accounting items, stock-based compensation, and legacy business wind-down costs, reflecting investment in building the company's communications infrastructure platform as it approaches scale efficiencies.
  • NIXX is -11.69% to $0.7995. 
  • Source: Press Release
